subject: Acid-Base
title: Acid-base disorders in 4 steps
hook: The approach that turns a scary ABG into easy marks. Swipe.

points.heading: Step 1: read the pH
points.body: Is the patient acidotic (pH below 7.35) or alkalotic (pH above 7.45)? This sets the primary direction before anything else.

points.heading: Step 2: find the driver
points.body: Look at CO2 and bicarbonate. In respiratory disorders the pH and CO2 move in opposite directions. In metabolic disorders the pH and bicarbonate move in the same direction.

points.heading: Step 3: check compensation
points.body: Is the other system compensating? Compensation shifts the values back toward normal but never overcorrects the pH past the midline.

points.heading: Step 4: anion gap
points.body: For a metabolic acidosis, calculate the anion gap. A high gap points to lactate, ketones, toxins or renal failure. A normal gap points to GI or renal bicarbonate loss.

caption: ABGs feel scary until you have a system. Read the pH, find whether CO2 or bicarbonate is driving it, check for compensation, then work the anion gap for a metabolic acidosis. Four steps, every time. Save this for placement. Recall Engine turns your respiratory and renal lectures into practice questions and flashcards. Start free, link in bio.
